description: Bacterial Spot of bell pepper is caused by Xanthomonas species. Small water-soaked spots appear on leaves and fruits, turning dark brown with yellow halos. Spread is favoured by warm, humid weather and overhead irrigation.
treatment: Apply copper bactericide (Copper Oxychloride 50WP at 3g/L) every 7 days. Use streptocycline at 200ppm as foliar spray. Remove and destroy heavily infected plant material immediately.
organic_alternative: Spray Bordeaux mixture (1%) at weekly intervals. Apply Pseudomonas fluorescens (10g/L) as foliar treatment. Avoid wetting foliage — switch to drip irrigation.
prevention: Use certified disease-free seeds or hot-water treated seeds (50°C for 25 min)
Avoid overhead irrigation — use drip instead
Practice 2-year crop rotation with non-solanaceous crops
Maintain proper plant spacing for good air circulation
Remove and bury infected plant debris after harvest
fertilizer: Apply balanced NPK 15-15-15 at 200kg/ha. Supplement with calcium nitrate (25kg/ha) to strengthen cell walls and reduce disease entry points.

description: Bell pepper plant is healthy with uniform green leaves, vigorous upright growth, and blemish-free fruits.
treatment: No treatment required. Continue regular monitoring.
organic_alternative: Apply compost tea biweekly as a preventive foliar spray to maintain plant immunity.
prevention: Continue regular scouting for early pest and disease detection
Maintain balanced fertilization to avoid nutrient stress
Ensure good air circulation with proper spacing
Use mulching to retain moisture and suppress weeds
fertilizer: Apply NPK 13-40-13 at transplanting, then switch to NPK 15-15-30 during fruiting for optimal yield.

description: Early blight is caused by Alternaria solani. Produces circular brown spots with concentric rings (target-board pattern) on older lower leaves first, then spreads upward. Common in warm, humid conditions with water-stressed plants.
treatment: Apply Mancozeb 75WP (2.5g/L) or Chlorothalonil 75WP (2g/L) at first symptom. Spray at 10-14 day intervals. Remove heavily infected lower leaves before spraying.
organic_alternative: Apply compost tea spray weekly. Use Bacillus subtilis-based biocontrol (5g/L). Mulch around plant base to prevent soil splash inoculum.
prevention: 3-year crop rotation with cereals or legumes
Remove all infected plant debris after harvest
Avoid water stress — maintain consistent irrigation
Use certified disease-free seed tubers
Apply adequate potassium fertilizer to strengthen plant tissues
fertilizer: Balanced NPK 15-15-15 at 200kg/ha. Apply potassium sulphate (50kg/ha) to improve leaf health and disease resistance.

description: Late blight is caused by Phytophthora infestans — the same pathogen that caused the Irish Potato Famine. Dark water-soaked lesions rapidly destroy foliage and cause tuber rot. Spreads explosively in cool (10-20°C), humid conditions.
treatment: Apply Metalaxyl-M + Mancozeb (Ridomil Gold MZ) at 2.5g/L at first sign. Hill up potatoes to protect tubers. Destroy infected haulms before harvest.
organic_alternative: Apply copper hydroxide (Kocide) at 3g/L — approved for organic use. Use row covers during wet periods. Remove and compost infected material away from field.
prevention: Plant only certified disease-free seed tubers
Avoid planting near tomato fields (same pathogen)
Destroy volunteer potato plants
Ensure well-drained soil — avoid waterlogging
Harvest promptly when mature; avoid leaving tubers in wet soil
fertilizer: NPK 12-32-16 at planting for strong root development. Avoid excess nitrogen. Apply calcium (gypsum) to soil to strengthen tuber skin.

description: Potato plant is healthy with lush green foliage, strong stems, and uniform tuber development underground.
treatment: No treatment required. Continue preventive monitoring.
organic_alternative: Apply Trichoderma viride (5g/L) as soil drench to maintain soil health and suppress latent pathogens.
prevention: Regular earthing-up to protect developing tubers
Scout weekly for early blight/late blight symptoms
Maintain consistent soil moisture — avoid drought stress
Monitor for Colorado potato beetle
fertilizer: NPK 120-80-80 kg/ha split into 3 applications. Top-dress with urea at earthing-up stage.

description: Tomato Bacterial Spot is caused by Xanthomonas vesicatoria. Produces small dark water-soaked spots with yellow halos on leaves, stems, and fruits. Spreads rapidly in warm, wet weather through rain splash and handling.
treatment: Apply copper-based bactericide (Copper Oxychloride at 3g/L) every 5-7 days. Combine with Mancozeb for better control. Avoid working in fields when foliage is wet.
organic_alternative: Spray Bordeaux mixture 0.5% at 7-day intervals. Apply neem oil (5ml/L) as protective spray. Use biological agent Bacillus amyloliquefaciens.
prevention: Use certified pathogen-free seeds — treat with hot water (50°C for 25 min)
Avoid overhead irrigation
Practice 2-3 year crop rotation
Sanitise all tools and equipment with bleach solution (1:10)
Remove infected plant material immediately
fertilizer: NPK 15-15-15 at 200kg/ha. Supplement with calcium nitrate (200g/100L water) as foliar to improve tissue strength.

description: Early blight caused by Alternaria solani shows characteristic target-like concentric rings on lower, older leaves first. Lesions are dark brown with yellow halo. Spreads upward and can cause significant defoliation in humid conditions.
treatment: Apply Chlorothalonil 75WP (2g/L) or Azoxystrobin 23SC (1ml/L). Begin at first symptom; repeat every 10-14 days. Stake plants to improve air circulation.
organic_alternative: Spray baking soda solution (1 tbsp per gallon water + few drops liquid soap). Apply compost tea biweekly. Mulch heavily to prevent soil splash of spores.
prevention: Apply thick organic mulch around base of plants
Mandatory 3-year crop rotation
Water only at soil level — avoid wetting foliage
Maintain adequate plant spacing (60-75cm)
Remove and destroy all infected lower leaves promptly
fertilizer: Balanced NPK 15-15-15 at 200kg/ha. Add MOP (muriate of potash) at 40kg/ha for improved leaf health and disease tolerance.

description: Late blight (Phytophthora infestans) produces dark water-soaked lesions on leaves, stems, and fruit. A white sporulating mass appears on leaf undersides in humid conditions. Spreads explosively — can destroy a field in days under cool, wet weather.
treatment: Apply Mancozeb 75WP (2.5g/L) or Cymoxanil + Mancozeb (Curzate) at 2.5g/L. Spray preventively every 7 days during cool/wet weather. Remove all infected plant parts immediately.
organic_alternative: Spray neem oil solution (5ml/L water) every 7 days. Apply Trichoderma viride (5g/L) as soil drench. Use copper hydroxide 53.8WP for organic certified control.
prevention: Practice 3-year crop rotation with non-solanaceous crops
Use certified disease-resistant varieties (e.g., Legend, Mountain Magic)
Avoid overhead irrigation — use drip irrigation
Maintain proper plant spacing (75-100cm) for air circulation
Remove all crop debris and volunteer plants after harvest
fertilizer: NPK 10-26-26 for improved disease resistance. Add calcium nitrate at 25kg/ha to strengthen cell walls. Avoid excess nitrogen fertilization.

description: Tomato Leaf Mold caused by Passalora fulva. Yellow patches appear on upper leaf surfaces with characteristic olive-green to grey-brown velvety mold growth on the undersides. Most common in greenhouses with high humidity above 85%.
treatment: Apply Chlorothalonil 75WP (2g/L) or Mancozeb 75WP (2.5g/L) every 7-10 days. Improve greenhouse ventilation immediately. Remove and destroy heavily infected leaves.
organic_alternative: Increase air circulation — open vents and reduce plant density. Spray potassium bicarbonate solution (5g/L). Apply copper-based fungicide for organic management.
prevention: Maintain greenhouse humidity below 85% using ventilation
Avoid overhead watering — water at soil level
Increase plant spacing to 75cm minimum
Use resistant varieties (Shirley F1, DRW 5981)
Regularly remove and destroy old leaves
fertilizer: Apply NPK 15-15-30 to promote strong plant growth. Calcium and potassium supplementation helps improve tissue resistance.

description: Septoria Leaf Spot (Septoria lycopersici) produces numerous small circular spots with grey-white centres and dark borders on lower leaves. Spreads rapidly in warm, wet conditions through rain splash and wind. Can cause severe defoliation.
treatment: Apply Mancozeb 75WP (2.5g/L) or Chlorothalonil at 10-14 day intervals. Begin at first appearance on lower leaves. Remove all spotted leaves before spraying.
organic_alternative: Remove and destroy infected lower leaves immediately. Apply copper-based fungicide (3g/L). Use compost tea as preventive foliar spray every 10 days.
prevention: Remove lower infected leaves as soon as spotted
Avoid overhead irrigation and minimize leaf wetness
Practice 3-year crop rotation
Stake plants to keep foliage off the ground
Remove all crop debris after harvest
fertilizer: NPK 15-15-15 balanced fertilizer. Avoid excess nitrogen which promotes dense, humid canopy. Supplement with potassium for stronger tissue.

description: Two-spotted spider mite (Tetranychus urticae) causes fine stippling and yellowing of leaves with characteristic webbing on leaf undersides. Thrives in hot (>28°C), dry conditions. Can cause rapid defoliation and yield loss.
treatment: Apply Abamectin 1.8EC (1ml/L) or Spiromesifen 240SC (0.75ml/L) as foliar spray. Rotate miticide classes to prevent resistance. Apply to undersides of leaves thoroughly.
organic_alternative: Spray neem oil (5ml/L) every 5-7 days targeting leaf undersides. Release predatory mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis) for biological control. Increase humidity around plants using overhead misting.
prevention: Maintain adequate plant moisture — mites prefer water-stressed plants
Avoid dusty conditions — dust suppresses natural predators
Monitor with yellow sticky traps
Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that kill natural predator mites
Remove heavily infested leaves and destroy
fertilizer: Balanced NPK with emphasis on potassium (NPK 15-10-30) to improve plant vigour and tolerance to mite feeding.

description: Target Spot caused by Corynespora cassiicola produces brown circular lesions with concentric rings on leaves, stems, and fruits. Distinguished from early blight by the absence of yellow halo and its occurrence on both young and old tissue.
treatment: Apply Azoxystrobin (1ml/L) or Boscalid + Pyraclostrobin (0.75ml/L) every 10-14 days. Begin application at first sign. Rotate fungicide groups to manage resistance.
organic_alternative: Apply copper hydroxide at 3g/L. Use Bacillus subtilis (Serenade) as a biological fungicide. Remove infected leaves and improve canopy airflow.
prevention: Crop rotation with non-solanaceous crops
Use resistant varieties where available
Avoid dense planting — maintain 75cm spacing
Remove infected crop debris promptly
Irrigate at soil level to keep foliage dry
fertilizer: NPK 15-15-15 with supplemental calcium nitrate. Avoid high nitrogen applications that encourage dense, humid canopy.

description: Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus (TYLCV) is transmitted by silverleaf whitefly (Bemisia tabaci). Infected plants show upward leaf curling, yellowing margins, stunted growth, and severe yield loss. No cure once infected — management focuses on vector control.
treatment: Control whitefly vector using Imidacloprid 17.8SL (1ml/L) or Thiamethoxam 25WG (0.3g/L). Remove and destroy infected plants immediately to reduce inoculum source.
organic_alternative: Apply neem oil (5ml/L) to control whitefly population. Use yellow sticky traps for monitoring and mass trapping. Install insect-proof netting (50-mesh) in nurseries.
prevention: Use TYLCV-resistant/tolerant varieties (Ty-1 gene varieties)
Control whitefly from nursery stage with systemic insecticide
Install reflective silver mulch to repel whiteflies
Use 50-mesh insect screens in nurseries
Remove infected plants and surrounding weeds immediately
fertilizer: Apply NPK 15-15-15 at standard rates. Avoid excess nitrogen which attracts more whiteflies. Potassium sulphate helps maintain plant vigour under virus stress.

description: Tomato Mosaic Virus (ToMV) causes mottled light and dark green patches on leaves with distortion, curling, and stunted growth. Spreads mechanically through infected tools, hands, and contaminated seeds. No chemical cure available.
treatment: No curative treatment. Remove and destroy all infected plants immediately. Disinfect all tools with 10% bleach solution or 70% alcohol between plants.
organic_alternative: Disinfect tools with milk solution (skimmed milk diluted 1:10) — proteins bind and inactivate virus. Plant resistant varieties. Remove weeds that may serve as reservoir hosts.
prevention: Use certified virus-free seeds — heat treatment at 70°C for 2 days
Plant only ToMV-resistant varieties (Tm-2 gene)
Disinfect tools and hands frequently during crop operations
Remove infected plants immediately and do not compost them
Control aphids and other insect vectors
fertilizer: Balanced NPK 15-15-15. Avoid excess nitrogen. Foliar application of micronutrients (Zn, B, Fe) helps maintain yield in mildly infected plants.

description: Tomato plant is healthy with deep green leaves, strong stems, uniform fruit set, and no visible symptoms of disease or pest damage.
treatment: No treatment required. Continue preventive management.
organic_alternative: Apply compost tea (1L per 10L water) as foliar spray fortnightly to boost plant immunity. Companion plant with basil to deter common pests.
prevention: Continue weekly scouting for early pest and disease detection
Maintain consistent drip irrigation to avoid moisture fluctuation
Monitor for early signs of Late blight in cool, wet weather
Prune suckers regularly to maintain open canopy
Stake plants to keep fruits off the ground
fertilizer: NPK 13-40-13 at transplanting. Switch to NPK 15-10-30 during flowering and fruiting. Calcium nitrate foliar spray (2g/L) fortnightly to prevent blossom end rot.
